narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, piecing together the chapters from your undergrad days through law school to predict if you'll conquer the bar exam. Your story starts strong with a solid fifth-decile ranking in your first-year law school grades, but it hits a tough turn by third year, where you landed in the bottom decile—that became the biggest signal pulling the prediction toward a 62% chance of not passing the bar, as it suggests the rigors of advanced coursework might have been an uphill battle. Layered on top, your first-year decile reinforced that early momentum didn't hold steady, making it the second strongest factor tipping the scales toward failure. Even with your upper-middle family income offering some stability, it surprisingly aligned with patterns of students who faced similar hurdles, adding to the concerns. Your LSAT score of 34 showed promise on paper, yet in this mix, it nudged the outlook downward rather than lifting it. Your undergrad GPA of 2.8 painted a picture of consistent but not standout academic performance, further dimming the projection. As a white male studying full-time, those details fit profiles the team has seen struggle more often than succeed in passing the bar, each pulling in the same direction except for your gender, which offered a slight counterbalance toward success. It's this combination—like a puzzle where the toughest pieces dominate—that led to the tough call of rejecting your application, not because you're incapable, but because the overall narrative the data tells whispers caution. You've got real strengths here, like that LSAT grit, and with targeted steps to boost those later deciles, your story could rewrite itself dramatically. Hang in there—this is just one chapter, and you're the author of the next.
